title: Sharing Your Code: Modules & Functions
beginnerExplanation: In Python, a function is a named block of code that does a specific job. Instead of writing the same code over and over, you write it once as a function and then 'call' it by name whenever you need it. A module is simply a file ending in `.py` that contains one or more of these functions. By putting functions into a module, you can share them across multiple different Python programs or even distribute them to other developers.
intermediateExplanation: Functions are defined using the `def` keyword, followed by the function name, parentheses `()`, and a colon. You can pass inputs called arguments into functions. To share these functions, you save them in a separate `.py` file (like `nester.py`). Other scripts can access this code using the `import` statement. When you import a module, Python creates a separate namespace for it, protecting your variables from being overwritten.
advancedExplanation: Under the hood, when a function is called, CPython creates a new frame object on the call stack. This frame stores local variables and parameters. In recursive functions (like `print_lol`), each recursive call pushes a new frame onto the stack. If the recursion is too deep without reaching a base case, Python raises a `RecursionError` to prevent stack overflow. Modifying `sys.setrecursionlimit()` can change the maximum depth, but the default limit is 1000.

realWorldAnalogy.title: The Royal Scribe's Recipe Book
realWorldAnalogy.description: Imagine a royal kitchen. Instead of the head chef dictating how to bake bread (the code) to every single helper every single morning, the head chef writes the recipe down once in a recipe book (the function). When a helper needs to bake bread, they simply look up 'bake_bread' in the recipe book (calling the function). The recipe book itself is stored on a designated 'Bread & Pastries' shelf (the module) containing all bakery-related recipes.

mentalModel: A magical portal where you throw in raw materials (arguments), the portal processes them inside a closed bubble (scope), and then spits out a finished product (return value).
visualDiagram:
graph TD
  Program -->|import nester| Module[nester.py]
  Module -->|Defines| Func[print_lol]
  Program -->|Calls nester.print_lol| Func
  Func -->|Recursion| Func
executionWalkthrough: 1. `import nester` -> Python loads the nester.py module and registers the 'nester' namespace.
3. `nester.print_lol(movies)` -> Pushes a new frame for print_lol to the call stack with parameter the_list=movies.
4. `isinstance(item, list)` is called on each item. For the nested list, `print_lol` is recursively called, pushing a second frame onto the stack.
5. When the nested items are printed, the recursive frame pops off the stack, and execution returns to the outer frame.

memoryState.note: Nested list passed to recursive function

commonMistakes: Calling a module function directly without its namespace prefix (e.g., calling `print_lol()` instead of `nester.print_lol()`).
Writing code in a module that runs immediately upon import (always wrap setup/testing code in a `if __name__ == '__main__':` block).
Infinite recursion: forgetting to define a base case, leading to a RecursionError.

debuggingMission.scenario: A wizard tried to use the `nester` module to print his spells, but Python says `NameError: name 'print_lol' is not defined`.
debuggingMission.explanation: Because we used `import nester`, the `print_lol` function is stored inside the `nester` namespace. We must prefix the function call with the module name: `nester.print_lol(spells)`.

practiceQuestions.question: If you import a module using `import nester`, how do you call the function `print_lol` inside it?
practiceQuestions.explanation: You use dot notation to access functions inside an imported module: `module_name.function_name()`.

challengeQuestions.question: What keyword is used in Python to define a new function?

projectExercise.title: Creating the Nester Module
projectExercise.description: Define a function named `print_lol` that accepts a single parameter `the_list`. Inside the function, loop through `the_list` and print each item.
requirements: Use the `def` keyword to declare print_lol.
Use a `for` loop to iterate through the_list.
Print each item using the print() function.

bossBattle.name: The Recursion Hydra
bossBattle.description: A mythical beast that grows new heads whenever you attempt to defeat it. Only a perfectly structured recursive base-case can slice through all heads at once!
bossBattle.mechanic: Write a recursive function that decrements the hydra's head count and returns once it reaches zero.

artifacts.name: The Recursion Ring
artifacts.description: A golden band shaped like an eating snake, granting the wearer the ability to recall functions within themselves.

artifacts.name: Scroll of Namespace Shielding
artifacts.description: A protective parchment that creates a barrier around variables, preventing names from clashing.

masteryChecklist: I can define a custom function using def.
I can pass arguments to a function.
I understand the difference between function parameters and arguments.
I can import a module and call its functions using dot notation.
I understand what a namespace is.
confusionPoints: What is the difference between a function argument and a parameter?
simplifiedExplanation: Functions are like recipes: you write down the instructions once and give it a name. Whenever you want to cook that dish, you just call it by name. Modules are like recipe books that hold multiple recipes so you can keep your kitchen clean and share them with other chefs.
hintSystem: Did you define the function with 'def' followed by a space and name?
Remember to put parentheses () after the function name.
Don't forget the colon : at the end of the def line.
stepByStepHints: Use 'def print_lol(the_list):' to start the function.
Indent the next lines by 4 spaces.
Write 'for item in the_list:' as the next line.

retentionSystem.revision1: What keyword is used to define a function in Python?
retentionSystem.revision2: What happens to variable values when a function returns?
retentionSystem.revision3: Explain what a namespace is in your own words.
retentionSystem.revision4: How do you access a function inside a module that was imported using `import nester`?

developerMode.industryUsage: Functions and modules are the building blocks of clean, modular software design. Every single production Python codebase leverages modules to organize code.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Recursive calls have stack overhead and a default depth limit of 1000 in Python. For heavy data processing, an iterative loop is preferred.
bestPractices: Always document your functions with docstrings describing parameters and return values.
Keep functions focused on a single task (Single Responsibility Principle).
antiPatterns: Using global variables inside functions (makes code hard to test and debug).
Importing everything using 'from module import *' (pollutes the namespace and can cause silent name conflicts).
developerMode.architectureNotes: Each function call creates a frame on the call stack containing local variables. The frame is destroyed when the function returns.
